subject: ali-yahya

sectors_excited.name: Decentralized infrastructure networks (token-incentivized physical infra: wireless, compute, storage)
sectors_excited.why: The FTX implosion reinforces that the durable value in this space is permissionless, decentralized infrastructure with real-world utility. Token incentives can bootstrap physical networks that compete with centralized incumbents. This is exactly what a long-horizon infra investor should be funding through the winter.

sectors_excited.name: Zero-knowledge proofs and ZK rollups
sectors_excited.why: ZK is the most important cryptographic primitive for both scaling and privacy. zkEVMs are maturing, and post-Merge the rollup-centric roadmap makes L2 execution the frontier where infra investment compounds.

sectors_excited.name: Modular blockchains and L2 scaling
sectors_excited.why: After the Merge, Ethereum's path is rollup-centric. Separating execution, settlement, and data availability is how the base layer scales without sacrificing credible neutrality. This is foundational, not speculative.

sectors_excited.name: Decentralized social and the ownership economy
sectors_excited.why: With centralized social platforms in turmoil, protocols that give users ownership of their identity and social graph — a composable, user-owned alternative — are compelling. This is the ownership-economy thesis applied to networks people actually use daily.

sectors_excited.name: Self-custody, wallets, and account abstraction
sectors_excited.why: FTX is the strongest argument yet for self-custody — not your keys, not your coins. The remaining bottleneck is UX. Smarter wallets and account abstraction are how we onboard the next wave of users without compromising on decentralization.

sectors_concerned.name: Centralized exchanges and CeFi custodians/intermediaries
sectors_concerned.why: FTX, Celsius, Voyager, BlockFi — opaque centralized custodians with commingled funds and hidden leverage are the defining failure of this cycle, with contagion still unwinding (Genesis/DCG stress in December). Counterparty risk is the dominant near-term danger, and it only sharpens the case for decentralization.

sectors_concerned.name: Algorithmic / under-collateralized stablecoins
sectors_concerned.why: Terra/UST showed that reflexive, under-collateralized designs collapse under stress. I'm skeptical of any stablecoin model not backed by transparent, sufficient collateral.

tokens_excited.name: ETH
tokens_excited.why: Post-Merge, Ethereum is the credibly-neutral settlement layer with improving monetary properties (lower issuance, staking yield). It is the asset most aligned with the infrastructure and L2 thesis, on a long time horizon rather than a price call.

tokens_concerned.name: Centralized-exchange tokens (exchange-issued tokens)
tokens_concerned.why: FTT's collapse exposed the reflexive fragility of exchange tokens propped up by opaque balance sheets. I'm broadly cautious on any token whose value depends on a centralized, non-transparent intermediary.

risk_regime.why: Near-term the market is clearly risk-off: macro tightening from the Fed's 2022 hiking cycle, plus acute contagion and counterparty risk from the FTX collapse and its ongoing fallout. But as a long-horizon venture investor, this is precisely when to deploy into fundamentals and builders. Net: cautious on prices and counterparties, constructive on building infrastructure — which I read as neutral overall.

notes: My logged record here is empty, so every item above is extrapolated from my known thesis as an a16z crypto infrastructure investor as of end-2022 — I have no verbatim quotes to ground these and have deliberately not fabricated any citations. Overall posture reflects a 'build through the winter' stance: decentralization is the lesson of the FTX cycle, and infrastructure (ZK, modular/L2, decentralized physical networks, self-custody UX, user-owned social) is where I'd concentrate conviction.
